post.title: Arrival Waves, Not Scarcity: Tur’s Rally Faces a Reality Check!!
post.excerpt: India’s pulses market is approaching a moment of truth. For months, rising prices in pigeon peas (Tur/Arhar) have been explained through narratives of tight supply and delayed harvests. Yet a closer reading of arrivals, imports, and stock positions suggests a...
post.body:
India’s pulses market is approaching a moment of truth. For months, rising prices in pigeon peas (Tur/Arhar) have been explained through narratives of tight supply and delayed harvests. Yet a closer reading of arrivals, imports, and stock positions suggests a different story—one where physical availability is steadily overpowering speculation. The core numbers are difficult to ignore. India’s 2025–26 Tur balance sheet remains broadly comfortable: production near 2.7 million tonnes, imports around 1.0 million tonnes, and opening stocks of roughly 0.7 million tonnes together create total availability close to 4.4 million tonnes. Against consumption of about 3.9 million tonnes, the projected carry-out of nearly 0.5 million tonnes does not resemble a shortage market. It reflects adequacy—perhaps even mild comfort. The global picture reinforces this calm. World production is estimated near 4.0 million tonnes, led by India, Myanmar, and African origins. Total supply approaches 4.8 million tonnes, comfortably above global consumption of roughly 4.1 million tonnes, leaving meaningful ending stocks. Crucially, India absorbs nearly 95% of global Tur consumption, meaning recent price behaviour cannot be attributed to an unseen surge in international demand. Market signals within India tell a similar story. Across Tur, Urad, Chana, Lentils, and Yellow Peas, mandi firmness has repeatedly been followed by swift cooling—a classic signature of arrival-time speculation rather than structural scarcity. With Myanmar exports normalising, African future supply intact, and domestic arrivals from peninsular India building pace, prices are likely to find an anchor in physical supply. Meanwhile, Chana and Lentils face pressure from Australian and Canadian inflows, while weak Yellow Pea demand adds to the softer undertone across the pulses basket.

Short-term disruptions—unseasonal rains, delayed harvesting, and temporary farmer holding— have certainly tightened sentiment. But sentiment alone cannot sustain a rally detached from supply mathematics. When essential food commodities begin to behave like speculative assets, policy attention becomes inevitable, and history shows that late participants often absorb the sharpest corrections. The outlook ahead is therefore nuanced. Until mid-June, strong arrivals and comfortable imports suggest a bearish-to-sideways phase. Beyond mid-June, seasonal stock drawdown and monsoon uncertainty could gradually restore bullish undertones. For traders, processors, stockists, and farmers, the lesson is enduring: discipline must follow data, not narrative momentum. Tur’s long-term fundamentals may still support strength. But the recent rally—and near-term correction—are reminders that in agricultural markets, fundamentals eventually reclaim centre stage. And when they do, adjustments are rarely gentle.
post.author: Know Your Commodity
